text: Most spatiotemporal running variables (contact time, flight time, stride time, duty factor, stride length, swing time) show trivial and non-significant associations with running economy when considered in isolation.
Higher cadence is associated with lower oxygen/energy cost (better running economy), with a small but significant effect.
Smaller vertical displacement (vertical oscillation) is moderately associated with better running economy.
Higher vertical stiffness is moderately associated with better running economy.
Higher leg stiffness is associated with better running economy.
Joint angles (ankle, knee, hip) at initial contact, midstance, or toe-off, as well as their range of motion, are not significantly associated with running economy.
Individual biomechanical variables can explain only 4-12% of between-individual variation in running economy, though combining multiple variables may increase explanatory power.
